# Questions in Livestream 222

Live Stream #222: The Master of All Classes

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=628" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:28</a> | Does the Red Sea crossing justify infant baptism? | He does not think that is a strong argument and says the analogy to circumcision is too weak. | He says the Red Sea argument resembles the circumcision argument because both appeal to whole-community participation, including infants. His objection is that circumcision admitted people into the covenant community without saving them, and the Red Sea generation likewise did not make the kind of personal promise that baptism signifies. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=920" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:20</a> | When Hebrew dropped the ghayin sound, how was the pronunciation preserved in Gaza? | He says the local place-name tradition preserved it. | He explains that Gaza was continuously occupied, so the local pronunciation tradition persisted even after Hebrew stopped using the ghayin sound. He compares this to English spelling, where written letters depend on inherited pronunciation conventions rather than mechanically determining the sound. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=1040" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:20</a> | How would David answer radical pacifists who say Jesus never talks about war in the New Testament? | He calls that an argument from silence and not enough to mandate absolute pacifism. | He rejects the reasoning as an argument from silence: the New Testament not discussing something directly does not automatically forbid it. Drawing on his Mennonite background, he says he heard pacifist arguments often but never found them persuasive as a blanket rule against all participation in war. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=1332" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:12</a> | Did your Mennonite friends reject self-defense, or only attacking first? | He says many of them rejected aggression but still allowed self-defense. | He says it depended on the person, but most Mennonites he knew would defend themselves even if they rejected war or aggression. He gives examples of pacifists who would not carry guns but might still use ordinary defensive force, and he treats self-defense as a debated issue within Anabaptist communities. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=1495" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:55</a> | Aren't Mennonites anti-alcohol? | No; he says they are not anti-alcohol. | He corrects the assumption by saying Mennonites are not anti-alcohol and may drink, sometimes too much. He contrasts them with Methodists, whom he identifies as the anti-alcohol group in the comparison. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=1696" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:16</a> | Why are people's heads shown in profile rather than front view in ancient art? | He ties that mainly to the limits and habits of the artistic media being used. | He says front-facing figures do exist, especially in plaster painting and in special cases like Bes, but they are rare. For cut relief and stone surfaces, profile views gave artists clearer detail, so the medium strongly shaped the conventional Egyptian way of portraying faces and bodies. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=2030" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:50</a> | How are geophysical survey methods such as seismic resistance and GPR used in ancient Near Eastern archaeology? | He says GPR and cesium magnetometry are important and very effective, while seismic and resistance are less central. | He says geophysical survey is used and important, especially ground-penetrating radar and cesium magnetometry. He gives the example of Pi-Ramesses, where a modern village sits on the site, so survey methods allow archaeologists to map buried remains without tearing down current buildings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=2369" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:29</a> | What was ancient Egypt's relationship with cats? | Cats were pets, village animals, hunting aids, and religiously associated with Bast or Bastet. | He says cats filled several roles at once: they were pets, free-roaming village animals, practical pest control, and animals associated with Bast or Bastet. He also says Egyptians trained cats for marsh hunting, where they helped flush birds from reeds for hunters. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=2720" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:20</a> | Why does my profile picture look bad when you highlight my comments on stream? | He says the masking and overlay setup in OBS is the cause. | He explains that OBS masks a very dark gray color for the stream overlay, so any matching gray in the profile image becomes transparent or distorted. His practical fix is to use a lighter gray or pure black instead of the almost-black gray that the mask is removing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=2991" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:51</a> | Who was the most incompetent or bafflingly stupid pharaoh in Egyptian history? | He nominates Ptolemy XIII. | He names Ptolemy XIII because he was young, foolish, and surrounded by advisers trying to keep him out of trouble. His main example is that Ptolemy XIII picked fights with the Romans despite being in no position to manage the consequences. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=3121" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:01</a> | Do we know when Amunherkhepshef's firstborn died? | He says the exact year is uncertain, though he gives a rough Ramses II range. | He says the child was probably very young, perhaps three to six years old, but the birth and death dates are not known. He places the likely range around Ramses II's 18th to 25th regnal years, depending on when Amunherkhepshef married and when the child was born. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=3306" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:06</a> | Can you recap the Matthew and Luke genealogy question and who the Hyksos were? | He says Matthew gives legal descent through Joseph, Luke gives Christ's genetic descent, and the Hyksos were Amorite Semites who formed Egypt's Fifteenth Dynasty. | In his quick recap, Matthew presents Jesus' legal inheritance through Joseph while Luke gives the genetic descent of Christ. He describes the Hyksos as Amorite Semites who migrated into Egypt earlier and then formed the Fifteenth Dynasty during the Second Intermediate Period before being expelled. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=3415" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:55</a> | How can I tell whether a college is a degree mill? | Check accreditation, academic requirements, oversight, and whether it appears on known degree-mill lists. | He says to check whether the school appears on degree-mill lists and whether its requirements are academically credible. Warning signs include extremely fast degrees, tiny PhD theses, missing prerequisites, and programs where a student can fail core work yet still receive the degree. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=4497" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:57</a> | Is Dr. Faulk a fan of Dune? | Yes; he says he is a big fan of Frank Herbert's original six-book series. | He says he loves the original Frank Herbert Dune series, including all six books and even Chapterhouse. He is much less enthusiastic about the Kevin J. Anderson continuation material, though he mentions having met Anderson years earlier. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=4781" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:41</a> | What does ABR stand for? | It stands for Associates for Biblical Research. | He identifies ABR as Associates for Biblical Research, then distinguishes criticism from hatred. He says he strongly dislikes some ABR behavior and claims ethical problems, but he still frames the issue in terms of loving neighbors and enemies rather than hating individuals. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=5019" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:39</a> | Is dealing with ABR or Rohl supporters more insufferable? | He says both are bad, but ABR has more institutional and financial power. | He says ABR and Rohl supporters are two sides of the same coin because both have slandered him and mishandled data in his view. The difference, he says, is that ABR has major funders and institutional leverage, so it can put pressure on schools, institutions, and churches. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=5207" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:47</a> | Are David Down's followers less vocal? | He says they are now largely absent because Down died years ago and his views have faded. | He says David Down died roughly two decades earlier and never had the same continuing following as some other revised-chronology figures. In his view, Down's Velikovsky-style views have mostly faded and have few active followers left. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=5242" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:22</a> | Is there a sound way to detect oxymorons in the Old Testament? | He does not know of an easy or reliable method apart from close reading. | He says he cannot think of a reliable detection method because oxymorons have to be noticed while reading or while examining a specific passage. He does not deny that they might exist, but he does not know of a searchable shortcut or clear method to find them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=5312" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:28:32</a> | Why is pride so irresistible for believers and non-believers? | He says sinful pride feels superior, distorts self-assessment, and corrodes community. | He distinguishes good pride, such as satisfaction in Christ or in work well done, from sinful pride. Sinful pride places excessive importance on one's achievements or status, gives a false self-assessment, makes people feel superior, and damages community life. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=5782" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:22</a> | Was throwing the food away just for show? | Yes; he says it preserved the recipient's dignity while still getting him food. | He says the food was carefully wrapped and placed upright, so throwing it away was staged rather than wasteful. The point was to let the person retrieve food without feeling that he had accepted a humiliating handout, preserving dignity while still providing help. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=5979" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:39</a> | What are your thoughts on the Frankfurt silver inscription? | He says it appears legitimate, comes from a real excavation, and is important early evidence of Christianity. | After looking it up, he identifies it as a third-century silver amulet from a Roman-era tomb in Frankfurt. He treats it as legitimate because it came from an archaeological excavation, and he finds its references to Jesus Christ, son of God, and Titus important evidence for early Christian texts circulating in that region. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=6176" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:56</a> | Could the banana farm on top of Avaris be bought out for archaeology? | He says Avaris is partly accessible through field rentals, but he would especially like to excavate the plantation over the likely Migdol area. | He notes that Avaris is a mudbrick site, making preservation difficult, and says many nearby fields can already be rented for seasonal excavation. The plantation he most wants opened is not the general Avaris area but a banana plantation near the south end of the old Pelusiac lagoon, where he thinks the Migdol area may be. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=6388" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:28</a> | Does Egypt have eminent domain like the United States? | Yes; he says Egypt has eminent domain, though it is used more for state projects and urgent rescue archaeology than ordinary digs. | He says Egypt has eminent domain and gives New Cairo or capital-area development as a recent type of use. For archaeology, he says it is used much less often, mainly in urgent rescue contexts such as West Thebes rather than ordinary research excavations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=6468" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:48</a> | Do we know where the Lion's Den is? | He identifies the fortress called the Lion's Den with Tell el-Borg on the Road of Horus. | He clarifies that this is not Daniel's lion's den but an Egyptian fortress name on the Road of Horus. Moving west to east, he places Tjaru at Tell Hebua, then the Lion's Den at Tell el-Borg, then Migdol, and notes that James Hoffmeier excavated Tell el-Borg. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=6541" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:01</a> | Was firstborn inheritance automatic in the ancient Near East, and what happened if an Egyptian father had only daughters? | He says Egyptian inheritance centered on a designated male inheritor and job titles, but women and daughters could inherit under certain family circumstances. | He says Egypt used a designated inheritor, generally the firstborn son, especially for inheriting the father's job or professional titles. Property could be divided, males had priority, but women could inherit; if only daughters remained, inheritance could go to the wife and children if the mother was alive, or to a brother if she was not. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=6897" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:57</a> | Did the wife have to bear a son to receive her husband's inheritance? | No; he says bearing any child could make her claim permanent. | He says the wife did not need a son specifically; bearing a daughter was enough. The decisive point was bearing a child, because childbearing made her claim on the husband's inheritance permanent in the Egyptian pattern he is describing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=7114" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:34</a> | Is Egyptian inheritance why God intervened with the daughters of Zelophehad? | Yes; he says they needed special dispensation because Israel was operating with Egyptian inheritance expectations. | He says the daughters of Zelophehad needed special dispensation because the Israelites were still operating with Egyptian inheritance assumptions. God's intervention overrides those inherited expectations and permits the daughters' claim. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=7154" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:14</a> | When God judges a sinful nation, are repentant believers within it spared? | Not necessarily; he says believers can still suffer collateral damage from the sins of their community. | He says Scripture presents God judging nations, cities, communities, and even the church, so there is community responsibility and liability. Repentant believers may still suffer because they remain part of the judged community, and he describes that as collateral damage from sin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=8057" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:17</a> | Where do you get your recipes from? | He says he uses many sources, including cookbooks, online recipes, and family recipes. | He says the source depends on the recipe, but he draws from a large cookbook collection, online recipes, and a smaller set of family recipes. The answer is practical rather than tied to one favorite source. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=8140" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:40</a> | Is fish also expensive in Vancouver? | Yes; he says fish is expensive there despite Vancouver being a port city. | He says fish is expensive in Vancouver even though, as a port city, it receives plenty of fish locally. His point is that the local supply does not make fish cheap in practice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=8175" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:16:15</a> | Do you like hibachi? | He likes the taste but not the cleanup. | He says hibachi food tastes good, but the cleanup is messy enough that he dislikes using it. His later comments also frame hibachi as inferior to the barbecue style he personally prefers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=8233" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:13</a> | What is hibachi? | He describes it as a small Japanese coal-based grill used for quick searing. | He describes a hibachi as a small cast-iron, charcoal-style grill suited to skewers, thin meats, and fast searing over hot coals. He says it can produce very good food if used properly, but it is easy to overcook food and the old units were hard to clean and prone to rust. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=8348" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:08</a> | Are you allowed to air fry on keto? | Yes; he says there is nothing wrong with air frying on keto. | He gives a direct yes: air frying is compatible with keto. He does not attach any special restriction beyond the usual need for the food itself to fit the diet. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=8365" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:25</a> | If someone is not a Christian and does not know where to go, what should they do? | He says they can go directly to Christ, repent, seek forgiveness, and then work out baptism and church afterward. | He tells the questioner that nothing prevents them from going directly to Christ, asking forgiveness, repenting, and deciding to live for him. He puts repentance first, then says baptism and finding a church are subsequent steps rather than prerequisites for coming to Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=8508" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:48</a> | What current academic work or recent digs can you share? | He says he is not doing digs now, plans to work on the Exodus book, and recently published on computational chronologies. | He says he is not currently doing digs because excavation now requires institutional affiliation and the proper structure to handle permits, finances, and bonds. His current academic direction is the Exodus book, and his most recent published work was a January paper on computational chronologies in archaeometry. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=8609" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:29</a> | What is your biggest accomplishment in archaeology? | He names his book on the Ark of the Covenant in its Egyptian context. | When the field is narrowed to archaeology, he names The Ark of the Covenant in its Egyptian Context as his biggest accomplishment. He says it was the book he wanted to write during his PhD work, with the doctoral research functioning as the contextual foundation for that project. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=8843" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:23</a> | What wine would you recommend for fish? | He recommends a white Bordeaux for cod and a rose or white zinfandel for smoked salmon. | He says wine recommendations depend on the food pairing. For cod, he recommends an aged white Bordeaux such as Mouton Cadet; for smoked salmon, he suggests rose or white zinfandel because the sweetness pairs with the smokiness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=8998" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:58</a> | What wine works with a charcuterie board? | He recommends a red Bordeaux or Beaujolais because the tannins pair well with the board. | He says charcuterie can go red or white, but red is more common. He recommends Beaujolais or a red Bordeaux because charcuterie boards include meat, olives, walnuts, almonds, and other tannic foods that pair well with tannins in red wine. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=9246" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:06</a> | Can wine be too aged? | Yes; he says over-aged wine oxidizes and loses quality, with different aging windows for whites, reds, and roses. | He says over-aged wine oxidizes, separates, goes sour, and loses quality, though it does not simply become vinegar. He gives different windows by type: white Bordeaux is best around five years and may be bad by ten, while red Bordeaux may be prime around twenty-five years. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=9426" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:06</a> | Are there good tasting wines for someone who dislikes dry, bitter wine? | He suggests starting with lower-tannin wines such as zinfandels and white wines, while noting tastes can change with age. | He says dislike of dry or bitter wine often relates to tannins and can change with age. His practical suggestion is to start with lower-tannin choices such as zinfandels and white wines, while leaving open that red wines may become more appealing later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=9550" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:10</a> | Is it possible to be Protestant and believe in the assumption of Mary? | Yes; he says nothing in Protestantism strictly prevents it, though Protestants generally do not believe that dogma. | He says a Protestant could believe Marian dogmas, including the assumption, without a strict contradiction in Protestantism. He distinguishes that possibility from normal Protestant belief, noting that Protestants generally accept Theotokos but do not usually accept perpetual virginity, immaculate conception, or the assumption. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=9669" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:09</a> | Are there good books comparing the cases for a global or regional flood? | He thinks books exist but advises doing a literature review and adding something new if writing on the subject. | He thinks the subject has been addressed before, though he does not track that literature closely. His advice is to do a literature review first, find what has already been argued on both sides, and only write the book if it adds something new. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=9778" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:58</a> | Can AI be used to help with a literature review? | He says he would not use AI for that because it hallucinates and does not read well enough. | He says he would not rely on AI for a literature review because it is poor at reading and can hallucinate or make false connections. He illustrates this with AI responses about his own book that confused authorship and invented misleading assessments. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=10106" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:48:26</a> | Can writing centers help with literature reviews? | Not generally; he says a literature review is an iterative process of following bibliographies from good sources. | He says writing centers generally are not the main help for literature reviews. The method is to start with a good book on the subject, read its bibliography, read those works, and continue following bibliographies until the material begins repeating and running out. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=10357" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:37</a> | Didn't Ramses II claim he birthed himself? | No; he says Egyptian divine-birth ideology is not the same as claiming to be self-born. | He rejects the claim because Ramses II's ordinary parentage was known. Egyptian kingship could use divine-birth narratives, but he says that is not the same thing as claiming to have literally birthed oneself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=10751" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:11</a> | Have you made anything functional with Arduino or something similar? | Yes; he uses ESP32s rather than Arduinos, and the cheese cave uses a commercial Inkbird controller. | He says he has made functional projects but prefers ESP32 boards over Arduinos because they have more functionality and lower power use while using similar software. For the cheese cave, though, he bought a commercial Inkbird programmable controller because that application needed reliability. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=10825" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:25</a> | Is Canadian bacon actually Canadian? | Yes; he says it is Canadian, though it is basically ham. | He says Canadian bacon is actually Canadian but is basically ham. He adds that he can buy it locally, but he generally does not because it is expensive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=10927" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:02:07</a> | Was Sarah a cruel slave owner, and what is Genesis 16-20 about? | He says Sarah seems cruel toward Hagar, the privilege framing is anachronistic, and the passage shows Abraham and Sarah trying to force prophecy outside God's plan. | He says Sarah's cruelty toward Hagar is not understated and that Hagar likely feared being beaten when told to return. He rejects modern privilege framing as anachronistic, and reads the larger passage as Abraham and Sarah trying to bring about God's promise through their own plan rather than waiting for God's way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=11283" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:03</a> | Are the descendants of Isaac and Ishmael still fighting in the Middle East today? | He rejects that as Islamic mythology rather than Jewish or Christian history. | He says the idea that Isaac's and Ishmael's descendants are still fighting today is Islamic mythology rather than Judeo-Christian history. In his account, the Ishmaelites were Midian-area, Bedouin-like traders who were later displaced by Arabian tribes and either absorbed into neighboring peoples or disappeared as a distinct group by around the eighth century. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=11444" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:10:44</a> | Why do Arabs hate the Jews? | He gives Islam and the Quran as his direct explanation. | His answer is very brief and direct: he attributes the hatred to Islam and specifically to the Quran. He does not develop the point further in this segment before moving on. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=11486" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:26</a> | Does your book on the Ark of the Covenant make the ark out to be an idol from a Catholic viewpoint? | No; he says the book treats the ark through Late Bronze Age and early Iron Age cultural context, not Catholic interpretation. | He says the book does not treat the ark through Catholic theology or later interpretive traditions, and it does not even pursue Talmudic interpretations. Its scope is the Ark of the Covenant through Late Bronze Age and early Iron Age cultural context, stopping there rather than applying later theological frames. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=11548" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:28</a> | What is the purpose of the biblical writers recording Abraham and Sarah's life? | He says the Abraham narrative frames God's one-sided covenant and promises to his people. | He says Genesis 12-24 frames God's covenant with his people. In the covenant ceremony, Abraham prepares the divided animals, but God is the one who passes through, showing that God takes on both the suzerain role and the promise-making burden rather than Abraham making equal reciprocal promises. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=11745" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:15:45</a> | At Jericho, why were animals inside the city when judgment came? | He says animals were normally brought inside walled cities for safety and for food during a siege. | He says animals were commonly brought inside walled cities at night because leaving them outside exposed them to predators, thieves, and raiders. During a siege, the practice also protected the animals as a food supply; in multi-level houses, animals could be kept on the lower floor while people lived upstairs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=11943" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:03</a> | What does secular law mean in the Bible, and are there sources to read about it? | He means customary laws that preexisted the covenant and were incorporated into biblical law; he mentions Richard Averbeck and commentaries as places to start. | He defines secular law as customary legal practice that existed in the land before the biblical covenant and was then incorporated into biblical law. He gives divorce, Hammurabi-like legal material, and slavery laws as examples of preexisting social law taken into the biblical narrative, and says Richard Averbeck and biblical-law commentaries are places to begin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=12158" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22:38</a> | Could the springs of the waters of Neftoah in Joshua be a reference to Memphis? | He says the etymology is sound and the suggestion is feasible. | He says the etymological proposal is sound, explaining it as a plausible development from the Memphis-related form once part of the name is removed. He considers the identification feasible, though he adds that it works less well for an early-date Exodus model. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=12239" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:59</a> | When is the new covenant declared? | He says it is declared in Jeremiah 31 and consummated through Christ's sacrifice. | He says the new covenant is declared in Jeremiah 31, not only after John the Baptist. He reads the sour-grapes language as a contrast with inherited consequences under Sinai, then says Christ's sacrifice is what consummates the covenant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=12496" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:16</a> | Can an abductive argument for God be made from the Exodus alone? | He dislikes abductive arguments and says an inductive argument from the Exodus evidence would be stronger. | He says abduction is useful for forming hypotheses but weak as an argument because it often shifts the burden of proof and rests on plausibility. If the Exodus evidence is strong, he says it should be framed inductively by weighing evidence and counterfactuals rather than abductively. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=12774" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:54</a> | Can miracles be used as an inductive argument for God? | He says it depends on the class of miracle; second-order miracles of timing are more evidential than first-order miracles that leave no trace. | He says miracles can have a phenomenology that may be used evidentially, but the type matters. First-order miracles that defy nature, such as walking on water, usually leave no usable trace, while second-order miracles of timing, such as a natural event occurring at the right time and place, can fit an inductive argument better. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=12893" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:34:53</a> | What is the biggest problem for a maximalist view of the Exodus and conquest narrative? | He says Moses' identity is a bigger issue than Moses' existence, and extreme maximalism can overread the text. | He says the harder problem is not simply whether Moses existed, because he thinks there is indirect evidence for a Moses figure, but identifying Moses historically. He also says maximalists can create problems by overreading the text, depending on how maximalist the claim becomes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=13131" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:38:51</a> | Is using indirect evidence the same as using abductive argumentation? | No; he says direct or indirect evidence can support either inductive or abductive arguments, depending on the argumentative structure. | He says indirect evidence is not automatically abductive because the argument structure is separate from the evidence type. Direct or indirect evidence can be used inductively or abductively; what matters is whether the argument weighs probabilities and counterfactuals or only asserts a plausible hypothesis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=13520" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:45:20</a> | How strong is the claim that the real Mount Sinai is in Arabia? | He calls the Arabia claim terrible and traces it to bad Ron Wyatt-style theorizing. | He calls the Arabia claim terrible and says it originates in Ron Wyatt-style theorizing. He lists objections including Nuweiba Beach not being the Sea of Reeds, the lack of reeds, the deep underwater terrain, the implausible wind requirements, and the split rock being misplaced relative to Kadesh Barnea. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=13857" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:57</a> | Of the proposed tombs of Jesus, is the Church of the Holy Sepulchre the closest candidate? | Yes; he says the recent archaeological evidence favors it over rivals like Gordon's Calvary. | He says the Church of the Holy Sepulchre is the best candidate because recent excavation found first- and second-century pottery suggesting pilgrimage activity, along with olive pits and pollen indicating a garden. He contrasts it with Gordon's Calvary, whose tombs he says are Iron Age and therefore much too old for Jesus' tomb. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=14110" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:55:10</a> | Are we assuming there was a garden there because the women thought the angel was a gardener? | Yes; he says you do not have gardeners where there is no garden. | He answers yes and treats the gardener detail as meaningful because gardeners imply the presence of a garden. He also accepts the later note that a Jupiter temple was built over the site after Jerusalem's destruction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=14165" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:56:05</a> | Was the Wailing Wall part of the Roman fortress Antonia? | No; he says it is part of Herod's retaining structure for the expanded plaza. | He says Antonia was north of the temple and looked down into it, while the Wailing Wall is part of Herod's expanded temple plaza. Herod built a retaining wall and filled behind it to extend the plaza southward, so the Wailing Wall belongs to that Herodian retaining structure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=14259" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:57:39</a> | Which tomb do you consider to be David's tomb? | He says he does not know that a securely identified Davidic tomb has survived and notes that Israelite royal burials may have involved cremation. | He says he does not know whether there is an identifiable Davidic tomb. His broader issue is that no Israelite royal tombs are securely confirmed, and he thinks the evidence may point toward cremation and unknown ash burials for many Judean kings rather than preserved monumental tombs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=14468" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:01:08</a> | Would Israelite kings be burned and randomly buried, or would they have family graves? | He says the evidence is uncertain, though Saul may have been buried under an oak or in a sacred grove setting. | He says the evidence does not let him decide between family graves and less formal burial patterns. For Saul, he notes burial under an oak and suggests this could have been a sacred oak grotto, a known kind of setting in the ancient Near East and Israel, but he keeps the conclusion uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=14528" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:02:08</a> | What is the significance of Troy and the Hittites in biblical historical context? | He says the connection is limited, though Troy matters as part of the broader collapse of the Hittite world. | He says Troy has limited direct biblical significance, but its fall belongs to the period when the Hittite New Kingdom was losing control. That collapse meant no large Hittite empire stood in Israel's way in Canaan, while later Neo-Hittite city-states appear only as smaller players in Kings and Chronicles. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=14705" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:05:05</a> | What are examples of ancient figures whose biographies were written after they died? | He says Roman emperors are an easy example, since later historians often wrote the surviving narratives. | He says just about every Roman emperor qualifies, pointing to Suetonius and other Roman historians who wrote after the emperors' deaths. He says postmortem writing was common because it was safer to discuss a dead ruler than a living one who could punish slander or criticism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Inh9XfP2T9w?t=15004" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:10:04</a> | How is your plague book coming along? | He says it is not the current project but is next in line after the video game work. | He says he has not worked on the plague book recently because other deadline projects are ahead of it. Once the video game work is finished, he expects the plague book to be the next major project. |
